content:
I am interested in classes of rings defined by properties, and would be interested in knowing what sort of tricks I can apply to deduce a certain class would be closed under tensor products. In particular for this conversation, just commutative rings suffices.

I have a great deal of difficulty reasoning about tensor products of rings, but a lot more experience checking if finite/infinite products of rings have a certain property.

In addition, if anyone's aware of literature that already collects results on stability of properties via the coproduct, I'd be interested in pointers.

Maybe you know that for any integer nnn, if you tensor a commutative ring where every element xxx has nx=0n x = 0nx=0 with another commutative ring, you get a commutative ring where every element xxx has nx=0n x = 0nx=0. So this property is not only closed under tensor products but "absorbing".
I think a class of commutative rings that's absorbing in this sense is often called a tensor ideal.

Also, any class axiomatized by 'existential sentences' in the language of rings (i.e. sentences saying there exist some elements x1,…,xnx_1, \dots, x_nx1​,…,xn​ satisfying some polynomial equations) is closed under ring homomorphisms, hence in particular under the map from R→R⊗SR \to R \otimes SR→R⊗S sending rrr to r⊗1r \otimes 1r⊗1. So, any such class is not only closed under tensor products, but is a tensor ideal.

@John Baez That reminds me that the tensor product of two rings can be the zero ring. This is a case where a "disjoint union" picture of coproduct isn't a good mnemonic :joy:

Some time ago we talked about equationally defined rings. I'm not sure this is the same thing though... only existential quantification? Or are there free variables in there that do the job of universal quantification? I was prepared for von Neumann regular rings to behave well under tensor product.

You shouldn't think of a coproduct of commutative rings as like a 'disjoint union'. You should think of a commutative ring as the ring of well-behaved functions on some interesting sort of space (called an affine scheme), and then the coproduct of commutative rings is the ring of functions on the product of such spaces.

The trick I mentioned let you show that any class of commutative rings defined by existentially quantified polynomial equations is a tensor ideal. This is not the case for every class of commutative rings defined by universally quantified polynomial equations. (These are called 'varieties' of commutative rings, and they're nice in many ways, but not this way!)
What are some examples of classes that are existentially quantified?
For instance the class of rings containing a solution to x2=−1x^2 = -1x2=−1, which include Z/2Z\mathbb{Z}/2\ZZ/2Z and C\mathbb{C}C but not Z\ZZ or R\RR

People define a divisible group to be an abelian group AAA such that for each nnn and each a∈Aa \in Aa∈A there exists b∈Ab \in Ab∈A such that

b+⋯+b=ab + \cdots + b = ab+⋯+b=a

where we add nnn copies of bbb. In other words, we can "divide aaa by nnn".

We can define a divisible ring to be a ring whose underlying additive group is divisible. For example Z\mathbb{Z}Z is not divisible since you can't divide by 222 in the ring of integers, but Q\mathbb{Q}Q is a divisible ring.

Divisible abelian groups are somewhat important in homological algebra since they're exactly the injective objects in the category of abelian groups (hmm, at least if you assume the axiom of choice, according to Wikipedia).

But it's a lot easier to see that divisible rings are an existentially quantified class, so they form a tensor ideal. The latter is easy to see directly: if we can "divide by nnn" in a ring AAA then you can divide by nnn in A⊗BA \otimes BA⊗B for any ring BBB.

I would not have expected the divisibility example, because I thought the universal quantifications weren't allowed. An von Neumann regularity isn't of the same type? For each aaa there exists bbb such that a=abaa=abaa=aba?

Ugh, you're right, there are universal quantifiers in the axioms

∀a∃b(a=2b) \forall a \exists b (a = 2b) ∀a∃b(a=2b)
∀a∃b(a=3b) \forall a \exists b (a = 3b)∀a∃b(a=3b)
∀a∃b(a=4b) \forall a \exists b (a = 4b)∀a∃b(a=4b)
etc.

(At least no universal quantification over nnn is needed! It's fine to have infinitely many axioms.)

However, I still believe that if RRR is a divisible commutative ring, and SSS is any commutative ring, then R⊗ZSR \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} SR⊗Z​S is divisible. Any element xxx of R⊗ZSR \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} SR⊗Z​S is of the form

x=r1⊗s1+⋯+rk⊗sk x = r_1 \otimes s_1 + \cdots + r_k \otimes s_kx=r1​⊗s1​+⋯+rk​⊗sk​

Choose n∈{1,2,3,… }n \in \{1,2,3, \dots \}n∈{1,2,3,…}. We can write ri=nri′r_i = n r'_iri​=nri′​. Thus

x=n(r1′⊗s1+⋯+rk′⊗sk) x = n (r'_1 \otimes s_1 + \cdots + r'_k \otimes s_k)x=n(r1′​⊗s1​+⋯+rk′​⊗sk​)

as desired. So R⊗ZSR \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} SR⊗Z​S is divisible.

So, divisible commutative rings form a tensor ideal - and thus they're closed under taking tensor products.

Does this look correct?

There's probably a lesson here that can be generalized to other classes of rings: some kinds of universal quantifiers are allowed, and we still get a tensor ideal. I will let someone else figure that out!

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

For instance the class of rings containing a solution to x2=−1x^2 = -1x2=−1, which include Z/2Z\mathbb{Z}/2\ZZ/2Z and C\mathbb{C}C but not Z\ZZ or R\RR

BTW i saw this one, and that makes sense, although the property is rather of a different flavor than the ones I think about :)

For physicists this property is a biggie. We use a wide diversity of rings in quantum physics - even von Neumann regular rings we invented for this purpose - but quantum physics would not fly without i.
I imagine number theorists often want an nth root of 1 for some purpose.

By the way, my sources provide an example showing commutative von Neumann regular rings are not closed under tensor product.

Q(t)\mathbb{Q}(t)Q(t) is a [[von Neumann regular ring]] (since it's a field), while

Q(t)⊗ZQ(t)≅Q(t)⊗QQ(t)\mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}(t) \cong \mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(t) Q(t)⊗Z​Q(t)≅Q(t)⊗Q​Q(t)

is not (since it's an [[integral domain]] but not a field).

I don't understand why Q(t)⊗QQ(t)\mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(t) Q(t)⊗Q​Q(t) an integral domain but not a field, but here's the argument that a von Neumann regular ring that's an integral domain is a field:

In an integral domain, if a≠0a \neq 0a=0 satisfies a=axaa = axaa=axa for some xxx , then axaxax is nonzero and it's an idempotent. But integral domains have only 000 and 111 as idempotents, so ax=1ax = 1ax=1, so aaa has an inverse.

I understand that at least!

John Baez said:

Does this look correct?

It does look correct! I just wish I knew what I could say about the properties I'm interested in. Let's suppose we were interested in some sort of domain. Then it seems like right away you'd have to restrict to some particular ring characteristic (or family of characteristics?) to keep the zero ring out of your class of domains.
My intuition for it is that VNR rings have lots of idempotents, so a ring like a domain with minimal idempotents is going to interact violently with the condition.

John Baez said:

We can define a divisible ring to be a ring whose underlying additive group is divisible.

It looks to me like this amounts to a Q\mathbb QQ algebra, yes?

Certainly any Q\mathbb{Q}Q-algebra will be a divisible ring, but I'm confused about the converse, because in the definition of divisible ring there's no guarantee that given aaa in the ring and n∈{1,2,3,… }n \in \{1,2,3, \dots \}n∈{1,2,3,…} there's a unique bbb with a=nba = nba=nb.

So I don't know if, given a divisible ring, there always exists a way to consistently choose how to divide ring elements by nonzero integers and obtain a Q\mathbb{Q}Q-algebra. I also don't if there's a unique way.

Now that I think about it, I'm guessing it's possible to prove these with a bit of work, e.g. showing a lemma that says a [[divisible group]] has no nnn-torsion elements (bbb with nb=0n b = 0nb=0), so we can't have nb=nb′n b = n b'nb=nb′ unless b=b′b = b'b=b′.

Yeah, that's where I was going to begin. There's no torsion elements, so in particular Z\mathbb ZZ is a subring of RRR matching the abelian group action. Then in particular you can divide 1 by any nnn to get inverses for the elements of Z\mathbb ZZ.
I haven't seen 'divisible rings' in literature but I have seen torsion-free rings (in the sense of no nonzero finite-order elements.)

I'll admit I've never seen divisible rings in the literature either, just divisible groups (which seems to usually mean divisible abelian groups). I just made up the concept.

Of course torsion-free groups are more general than divisible groups, and ditto for rings.

Divisible rings RRR are the same as Q\mathbb{Q}Q-algebras.

Start with the fact that an abelian group is divisible iff it is injective. Injectivity of RRR implies that the unique ring map Z→R\mathbb{Z} \to RZ→R, taking 111 to 1R1_R1R​, can be extended to an abelian group map f:Q→Rf: \mathbb{Q} \to Rf:Q→R.

Claim: For all nonzero integers nnn, f(n)=n⋅1Rf(n) = n \cdot 1_Rf(n)=n⋅1R​ is invertible in RRR, with inverse f(1n)f(\frac1{n})f(n1​). Consequently, RRR is torsionfree.

Proof: 1R=f(1)=f(n⋅1n)=nf(1n)=n1Rf(1n)=f(n)f(1n)1_R = f(1) = f(n \cdot \frac1{n}) = n f(\frac1{n}) = n 1_R f(\frac1{n}) = f(n) f(\frac1{n})1R​=f(1)=f(n⋅n1​)=nf(n1​)=n1R​f(n1​)=f(n)f(n1​)

where the third equality uses only the fact that fff is an additive homomorphism.

Claim: f:Q→Rf: \mathbb{Q} \to Rf:Q→R is a ring map.

Proof: Additivity and preservation of the unit 111 were baked in from the outset. To check preservation of multiplication, f(a/b⋅c/d)=f(a/b)f(c/d)f(a/b \cdot c/d) = f(a/b) f(c/d)f(a/b⋅c/d)=f(a/b)f(c/d), it suffices to check that multiplication of each side by bdbdbd gives an equality, since multiplication by bdbdbd is an injective map R→RR \to RR→R, by the fact that RRR is torsionfree. But the result is indeed an equality since it is just f(ac)=f(a)f(c)f(a c) = f(a) f(c)f(ac)=f(a)f(c), and the restriction of fff to integers is a ring map by definition of fff.

Very nice, @Todd Trimble!

One spinoff is that 'being a Q\mathbb{Q}Q-algebra', while superficially a structure on a ring, turns out to be a mere property: i.e., a ring can have this structure in at most one way.

One can ask for which commutative rings RRR is 'being an RRR-algebra' a mere property. Apparently these are the [[solid rings]]: rings RRR such any two ring homomorphisms f,g:R→Sf, g: R \to Sf,g:R→S to any other ring must be equal.

So, I believe that RRR-algebras for any solid commutative ring RRR form a full subcategory of the category of commutative rings that is closed under tensor product (as @Ryan Schwiebert wanted) but also in fact a tensor ideal.

A while back I added to the nLab the complete classification of solid rings. Someone also put in a proof that Q\mathbb{Q}Q is a solid ring - maybe @Todd Trimble?
It wasn't me. I didn't scour the history, but the way it's written makes it look like Urs put that in.

John Baez said:

By the way, my sources provide an example showing commutative von Neumann regular rings are not closed under tensor product.

Q(t)\mathbb{Q}(t)Q(t) is a [[von Neumann regular ring]] (since it's a field), while

Q(t)⊗ZQ(t)≅Q(t)⊗QQ(t)\mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}(t) \cong \mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(t) Q(t)⊗Z​Q(t)≅Q(t)⊗Q​Q(t)

is not (since it's an [[integral domain]] but not a field).

I don't understand why Q(t)⊗QQ(t)\mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(t) Q(t)⊗Q​Q(t) an integral domain but not a field, but here's the argument that a von Neumann regular ring that's an integral domain is a field:

In an integral domain, if a≠0a \neq 0a=0 satisfies a=axaa = axaa=axa for some xxx , then axaxax is nonzero and it's an idempotent. But integral domains have only 000 and 111 as idempotents, so ax=1ax = 1ax=1, so aaa has an inverse.

I understand that at least!

About that last part: Q(t)⊗QQ(t)≅Q(x)⊗QQ(y)\mathbb{Q}(t) \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(t) \cong \mathbb{Q}(x) \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{Q}(y)Q(t)⊗Q​Q(t)≅Q(x)⊗Q​Q(y) is embedded inside the field Q(x,y)\mathbb{Q}(x, y)Q(x,y) of rational functions in x,yx, yx,y, being the subring of rational functions of the form ∑i=1npi(x)qi(y)\sum_{i=1}^n p_i(x) q_i(y)∑i=1n​pi​(x)qi​(y) where pi(x)∈Q(x)p_i(x) \in \mathbb{Q}(x)pi​(x)∈Q(x) and qi(y)∈Q(y)q_i(y) \in \mathbb{Q}(y)qi​(y)∈Q(y) are rational functions in their variables. Clearly

Q(x)⊗QQ(y)⊆Q(x,y)\mathbb{Q}(x) \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{Q}(y) \subseteq \mathbb{Q}(x, y)Q(x)⊗Q​Q(y)⊆Q(x,y)

is a strict inclusion, and so (1) the subring is an integral domain, being a subring of a field, and (2) the subring is not itself a field: consider the strict inclusions

Q[x,y]≅Q[x]⊗QQ[y]⊂Q(x)⊗QQ(y)⊂Q(x,y)\mathbb{Q}[x, y] \cong \mathbb{Q}[x] \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{Q}[y] \subset \mathbb{Q}(x) \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{Q}(y) \subset \mathbb{Q}(x, y)Q[x,y]≅Q[x]⊗Q​Q[y]⊂Q(x)⊗Q​Q(y)⊂Q(x,y)

where Q(x,y)\mathbb{Q}(x, y)Q(x,y) is the field of fractions of Q[x,y]\mathbb{Q}[x, y]Q[x,y] and hence also of the intermediate ring Q(x)⊗QQ(y)\mathbb{Q}(x) \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{Q}(y)Q(x)⊗Q​Q(y), but since this ring is properly contained in its field of fractions, it is not itself a field.

Ryan Schwiebert said:

John Baez said:

We can define a divisible ring to be a ring whose underlying additive group is divisible.

It looks to me like this amounts to a Q\mathbb QQ algebra, yes?

This is a good observation, because this recharacterization of divisible rings as Q\mathbb{Q}Q-algebras is clearly axiomatized by existential sentences (there exists an inverse of 2, there exists an inverse of 3, ...).

Oh good, so this shows divisible rings are axiomatized by existential sentences!

(And so, for people losing track of the plot here, they form a tensor ideal by a general theorem I mentioned earlier.)
I'm intrigued by these tensor ideals. I've not thought much about them. Can you tell me where they are studied, John?
Regarding n-divisibility for less than all n's, one could phrase a bunch of theorems about group algebras in terms of "n divisibility," e.g. Maschke's theorem.
Being a tensor ideal must be of a wildly different sort of character than stability with products. It looks like commonplace classes of rings have almost nothing to do with tensor ideals.

Todd Trimble said:

I'm intrigued by these tensor ideals. I've not thought much about them. Can you tell me where they are studied, John?

The closest thing I know is know comes from Paul Balmer's work on tensor-triangular geometry, founded in his 2005 paper The spectrum of prime ideals in tensor triangulated categories. They are working with tensor-triangulated categories, and their ideals are 'thick ⊗-ideals': full triangulated subcategories closed under tensoring with any object. Balmer and a bunch of folks have developed this idea a lot. But their setting is triangulated, not CommRing... and I don't really know anything about their work!

In reality, I thought of 'tensor ideals' in response to Ryan's original question merely because of our own work on 2-ideals in 2-rigs.
This result about von Neumann regular rings is quite surprising to me, because it means that the product of two zero-dimensional schemes can fail to be zero-dimensional.

So many of the properties I usually think about are amenable to finite or arbitrary products. I think the discussion above is showing the stuff that survives coproducts is of quite a different flavor.

@Graham Manuell - are you implicitly saying that an affine scheme is zero-dimensional iff its corresponding commutative ring is a von Neumann regular ring? I hadn't known that (since I don't even know any definition of "zero-dimensionality" for schemes, being pretty ignorant about schemes).
I think von Neumann regular corresponds to zero-dimensional + reduced. Usually people use the Krull dimension. In this case it should be the same as the spectrum having a base of clopens.

I just learned the following remarkable fact, due to someone named Rodney Sharp:

  • Let K,LK, LK,L be two extension fields of a field kkk. Then KrullDim(K⊗kL)=min⁡(Tr(K/k),Tr(L/k))\mathrm{KrullDim}(K \otimes_k L) = \min(\mathrm{Tr}(K/k), \mathrm{Tr}(L/k))KrullDim(K⊗k​L)=min(Tr(K/k),Tr(L/k))

(where we declare the Krull dimension to be ∞\infty∞ if it is not finite, and the transcendence degree Tr(K/k)\mathrm{Tr}(K/k)Tr(K/k) to be ∞\infty∞ if it is not finite).

So the dimension of a product of zero-dimensional schemes can be as big as you please, even ∞\infty∞, in this sense.

I mentioned this on Mastodon giving the example of the ring R⊗QR\mathbb{R} \otimes_\mathbb{Q} \mathbb{R} R⊗Q​R, and someone pointed out that Dehn invariants of polyhedra live in the underlying abelian group of this mod 2πZ2\pi \mathbb{Z}2πZ. No idea if that connection is interesting.
Seems kind of interesting. I discovered a MO post that touches on this, here.
